title: CEA Economic Report of the President 2026 - The Labor Market
content: Chapter 14 of the 2026 Economic Report of the President, prepared by the Council of Economic Advisers, discusses how the Administration reads the U.S. labor market. The CEA reports the headline unemployment rate for the full population alongside the prime-age unemployment rate, which covers those age 25 to 54 years, and describes the unemployment rate as relatively low or high by historical standards rather than pointing at a single threshold. The Report benchmarks recent unemployment against average rates outside recessions as assessed by the National Bureau of Economic Research, treating the NBER business-cycle chronology as the reference for whether a period is expansionary or contractionary. The CEA also tracks the Labor Force Participation Rate for the full adult population and for the prime-age segment, noting that the overall LFPR is weighed down by the retirement of the Baby Boom cohorts and that the steepest age-specific declines in participation occur between age 62 and age 66. Employment trends are reviewed across industry supersectors so that broad-based strength or weakness in hiring can be distinguished from sector-specific moves. The incidence of hiring, voluntarily quitting, and layoffs is compared with pre-pandemic benchmarks rather than month-to-month swings. The Report's framework implies that reading the unemployment rate in real time means placing the current level against the historical non-recession average, comparing prime-age and total rates, checking participation, and looking at which supersectors are driving the move. That is the federal executive branch interpretation of how the unemployment series should be read as a business-cycle indicator, and it is the framing against which sharp or persistent moves in the series are judged.
